| Hot (BOFU) | "You've seen the pattern. Now fix it." | "You've seen the pattern 3 times now. If you're ready to fix it, DM me." |
 
**Doctor Project adaptation:**
- TOFU: "Download the business model validation checklist." (free, zero friction)
- MOFU: "If you've already done a stakeholder audit, here's the architecture framework."
- BOFU: "If you've been following this for a while and it keeps resonating, your program probably needs diagnosis. DM me."
 
---
 
### Reciprocity-First CTA Pattern
 
**Principle:** Give genuine, high-value content before any ask. Audiences who have received real value feel a natural inclination to reciprocate through engagement or conversion.
 
**Rules:**
- Always give before asking -- the CTA comes after the value has been delivered
- The free resource must be genuinely valuable, not a thinly veiled pitch
- Document strategies and frameworks openly -- transparency activates reciprocity
- The bigger the free value, the stronger the reciprocity response
 
**CTA Formulas:**
 
| Approach | Formula | Example |
| -------- | ------- | ------- |
| Resource give | "I built [free resource] for you. [Where to get it]." | "I built the 3-week diagnosis framework for you. Link in comments." |
| Strategy share | "Here's the exact [approach] I use. If you want to apply it, [next step]." | "Here's the exact alignment matrix we use. If you want to apply it to your program, DM me." |
| Transparent breakdown | "I broke down [strategy/result] in full detail above. If this helped, [soft reciprocity ask]." | "I broke down our entire diagnosis process above. If this was useful, share it with a CIO who needs it." |
 
**Doctor Project adaptation:**
- Share complete frameworks in the post body, then: "If this framework helped, here's where to go deeper: [link or DM]."
- After posting a full case study breakdown: "If you want this diagnosis framework for your own program, DM me."
 
---
 
### Scarcity CTA Pattern
 
**Principle:** Limited availability increases perceived value and drives immediate action. Genuine scarcity is more powerful than manufactured urgency.
 
**Rules:**
- Only use scarcity when it is real -- fake countdown timers destroy trust
- Concrete limits outperform vague urgency ("only 5 spots" beats "limited availability")
- Application-based access creates exclusivity that increases desirability
- Waitlists and early access structures build anticipation before an offer launches
 
**CTA Formulas:**
 
| Type | Formula | Example |
| ---- | ------- | ------- |
| Capacity limit | "I only take [N] clients per [period]. [N-X] spots left." | "I only take 4 diagnostic engagements per quarter. 1 spot left this quarter." |
| Time-limited | "This [offer/resource] is only available until [date/event]." | "This offer is only available until the end of March." |
| Application gate | "This engagement is by application. If you think you qualify, DM me." | "Diagnosis engagements are by application. If your program is €20M+, DM me." |
| Waitlist | "I'm opening [offer] in [timeframe]. Get on the waitlist now." | "I'm opening 2 diagnostic spots in Q2. Get on the waitlist now." |
| Exclusive access | "First [N] to DM get [bonus/priority]." | "First 3 to DM get a free 30-min architecture audit before we start." |
 
**Doctor Project adaptation:**
- "I only run 4 diagnostic engagements per quarter. If you're 18 months in and off track, DM me before the Q2 slots fill."
- Application-based: "Diagnostic engagements are selective. If your program is €20M+ and visibly off track, apply by DM."
 
---
 
## CTA Type Library
 
*Extracted from Doctor Project CTA Guidelines (February 2026)*
 
### Ultimate CTA Checklist (Pre-Publish)
 
Before publishing any post, verify the CTA meets all criteria:
 
**Structure:**
- [ ] Contains only ONE call-to-action
- [ ] Adapted to audience maturity level (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU)
- [ ] Addresses the reader directly
- [ ] Includes an action verb
- [ ] Is in imperative form
 
**Clarity:**
- [ ] Uses accessible vocabulary (no consultant jargon)
- [ ] The requested action is specific
- [ ] The requested action is immediately achievable
 
**Impact:**
- [ ] Centered on a transformation promise
- [ ] Creates a sense of urgency
- [ ] Is visually highlighted (separated from post body)
 
---
 
### 20 Ready-to-Use CTAs (Doctor Project)
 
#### TOFU CTAs -- Soft (Drive Engagement)
 
Purpose: Build engagement, gather insights, start conversation.
Funnel stage: 50% of content (educational posts, thought leadership)
 
1. "What's your experience with MACH architecture? Comment below."
2. "Like if you've seen this pattern in your programs."
3. "Drop a like if you agree."
4. "MACH or monolith -- what's your take?"
5. "Subscribe if you want more architecture insights."
6. "If you're team 'validate before deploy,' drop a like."
7. "What's your 4th signal of architecture misalignment?"
8. "Which framework do you use to validate business model alignment?"
9. "If this resonates, let me know in the comments."
10. "Curious about your perspective. Agree or disagree?"
 
#### MOFU CTAs -- Medium (Drive Deeper Engagement)
 
Purpose: Offer resources, frameworks, deeper content.
Funnel stage: 30% of content (framework posts, case studies)
 
11. "Share this framework with CIOs who need it."
12. "If you want to fix architecture misalignment once and for all, here's the solution:"
13. "Need a fast validation framework? Here's what you need:"
14. "Download the business model alignment framework here:"
15. "Want the full diagnostic framework? Link in comments."
16. "If you're looking for a solution to stakeholder misalignment, I have what you need:"
 
#### BOFU CTAs -- Strong (Drive Conversion)
 
Purpose: Convert CIOs to clients. Drive DMs, calls, engagement.
Funnel stage: 20% of content (promotional posts, case studies with offers)
 
17. "If your program is off track, we should talk. DM me."
18. "If you're 18 months in and visibly behind, DM me."
19. "Need independent diagnosis in 3 weeks? DM me."
20. "If this sounds like your program, we should talk. DM me."
 
---
 
### Funnel-Stage CTA Match
 
| Stage | % of content | Post type | CTA strength | Example |
| ----- | ------------ | --------- | ------------ | ------- |
| TOFU | 50% | Educational, thought leadership | Soft (comment, like) | "What's your experience? Comment below." |
| MOFU | 30% | Framework posts, case studies | Medium (download, link) | "Want the full framework? Link in comments." |
| BOFU | 20% | Promotional, case studies with offers | Strong (DM, book call) | "If your program is off track, DM me." |
 
---
 
### CTA Clarity Rules
 
**DO:**
- Use action verbs (DM, comment, download, book)
- Be specific ("DM me" not "reach out")
- Make it low-friction (one click, no forms)
- Create urgency tied to the reader's situation ("If you're 18 months in and off track")
 
**DON'T:**
- Use consultant jargon ("Let's discuss strategic alignment")
- Ask for multiple actions ("Comment, like, share, DM")
- Make it vague ("Let me know your thoughts")
- Make it high-friction ("Fill out this 10-question form")
 
---
 
### CTA Transformation Promise Formula
 
**Weak CTA:**
"Want to learn more about architecture?"
 
**Strong CTA:**
"If you want to diagnose why your €50M program is off track in 3 weeks (not 6 months), DM me."
 
Why the strong CTA wins:
- Specific outcome (diagnose program)
- Quantified benefit (3 weeks vs. 6 months)
- Clear urgency tied to real situation (€50M program off track)
- Low friction (DM me)
 
The formula: [If you want to + specific outcome] + [quantified advantage] + [low-friction action].
`,
 
  "library/templates/linkedin-post/strong-opinion.md": `---
name: strong-opinion
source: original
content-types: [linkedin-post]
best-for-pillars: [1, 2, 4]
best-for-angles: [contrarian, observation]
date-created: 2026-02-24
---
 
# Strong Opinion Template
 
## When to Use
 
Use this template when you have a clear, bold stance on a common practice or belief in e-commerce, IT delivery, or consulting. The goal is to make the reader stop scrolling because you said something they secretly agree with but nobody dares say out loud.
 
Best suited for:
- Challenging conventional wisdom (fake agile, team-selling consulting model)
- Calling out costly mistakes decision-makers keep making
- Establishing authority through directness
 
## Specs
 
- **Length**: 600-900 characters
- **Tone authority level**: High (expert speaking from experience, not theory)
- **Visual rhythm**: Short blocks, maximum white space, 1-3 lines per paragraph
- **Symbols allowed**: \`->\`, \`>\`, \`-\` for lists
- **Emojis**: Zero
 
## Structure
 
\`\`\`
[HOOK — 3 lines, tension]
Line 1: <= 50 chars — Pain statement or bold claim
Line 2: <= 50 chars — Consequence or reinforcement
Line 3: <= 30 chars — Punch or pivot
 
[BOLD STATEMENT — 1 to 2 lines]
One sentence that stakes your position clearly.
No hedging. No "it depends."
 
[DEMONSTRATION — 3 to 5 lines with concrete examples]
Back your claim with observable patterns.
Use specific scenarios, numbers, or field observations.
Show the mechanism: why this happens, what it costs.
 
[INSIGHT — 1 to 2 lines]
The deeper truth behind the demonstration.
What the reader should now understand differently.
 
[CONCLUSION — punch line]
One strong closing statement.
Never a question. Never a social CTA.
\`\`\`
 
## Example Skeleton
 
\`\`\`
A senior costs less
than a poorly managed junior team.
It's math.
 
Most companies optimize for hourly rate.
They should optimize for delivery speed.
 
A junior at 400/day who needs 3 months of ramp-up,
2 rounds of rework, and a senior to review everything
costs more than a senior at 900/day who ships in 6 weeks.
 
The real cost isn't the daily rate.
It's the time lost making the wrong choice.
 
Staffing is a strategic decision. Not a purchase.
\`\`\`
`,
 
  "library/templates/linkedin-post/structured-analysis.md": `---
name: structured-analysis
source: original
content-types: [linkedin-post]
best-for-pillars: [1, 3, 5]
best-for-angles: [analytical, actionable]
date-created: 2026-02-24
---
 
# Structured Analysis Template
 
## When to Use
 
Use this template when breaking down a complex problem into clear, numbered points. The reader should walk away with a framework they can apply. This is the "depth and rigor" template -- it signals analytical thinking and rewards the reader with actionable structure.
 
Best suited for:
- Architecture decisions with multiple factors
- Product data governance breakdowns
- AI implementation analysis
- Any topic where 2-4 distinct points build a coherent argument
 
## Specs
 
- **Length**: 1,200-1,500 characters
- **Tone authority level**: High analytical (data-driven, structured, precise)
- **Visual rhythm**: Numbered or bulleted points, 1-2 lines each, white space between blocks
- **Symbols allowed**: \`->\`, \`>\`, \`-\`, numbered lists
- **Emojis**: Zero
 
## Structure
 
\`\`\`
[HOOK — 3 lines, tension]
Line 1: <= 50 chars — Surprising stat or pain statement
Line 2: <= 50 chars — Why it matters
Line 3: <= 30 chars — Pivot to analysis
 
[PROBLEM CONTEXT — 2 to 3 lines]
Set the scene. What decision or situation triggers this problem?
Frame it so the reader recognizes their own reality.
 
[POINT 1 — 2 lines]
First dimension of the analysis.
Concrete, specific, backed by logic or observation.
 
[POINT 2 — 2 lines]
Second dimension.
Build on Point 1 or add a complementary angle.
 
[POINT 3 — 2 lines]
Third dimension.
Often the non-obvious insight that ties it together.
 
[STRATEGIC INSIGHT — 2 to 3 lines]
The meta-lesson. What connects these points?
What should the reader now prioritize differently?
 
[CONCLUSION — punch line]
One strong closing statement.
 
[OPTIONAL CTA — subtle, strategic]
If relevant: "If you're in the middle of [X], this is worth auditing."
Never social. Never "like if you agree."
\`\`\`
 
## Example Skeleton
 
\`\`\`
70% of e-commerce migrations fail.
Not because of the technology.
Because of scoping.
 
Most companies treat migration as a technical project.
It's a business transformation with technical components.
 
1. They underestimate data complexity.
Product data spread across 4 systems with no single source of truth.
 
2. They skip the business process audit.
The old workflows get replicated in the new system -- bugs included.
 
3. They staff for build, not for governance.
Day 1 after go-live, nobody owns the data model.
 
The technology is rarely the bottleneck.
The bottleneck is the decisions made before a single line of code.
 
Architecture > Tools. Every time.
 
If you're planning a migration in 2026, audit your scoping first.
\`\`\`
`,
 
  "library/templates/linkedin-post/field-storytelling.md": `---
name: field-storytelling
source: original
content-types: [linkedin-post]
best-for-pillars: [2, 4]
best-for-angles: [observation, field-based]
date-created: 2026-02-24
---
 
# Field Storytelling Template
 
## When to Use
 
Use this template when you have a real (anonymized) client situation that illustrates a common mistake or pattern. The narrative arc creates engagement and the concrete details build credibility. This is the "I've been in the room" template.
 
Best suited for:
- Project failure post-mortems
- Delivery lessons learned
- Business-IT misalignment stories
- Consulting model critiques through real examples
 
## Specs
 
- **Length**: 1,400-1,800 characters
- **Tone authority level**: High experiential (senior practitioner sharing patterns)
- **Visual rhythm**: Short paragraphs, narrative flow, white space between scenes
- **Symbols allowed**: \`->\`, \`-\`
- **Emojis**: Zero
 
## Structure
 
\`\`\`
[HOOK — 3 lines, tension]
Line 1: <= 50 chars — Intriguing opening or pattern statement
Line 2: <= 50 chars — Stakes or consequence hint
Line 3: <= 30 chars — Pull into the story
 
[INITIAL SITUATION — 2 to 3 lines]
Set the scene with an anonymized client.
Industry, company size, project type.
Keep it recognizable to the target audience.
 
[WHAT HAPPENED — 3 to 5 lines]
The mistake, the decision, the problem.
Be specific about what went wrong and why.
Show the mechanism, not just the symptom.
 
[CONSEQUENCE — 2 to 3 lines]
Concrete business impact.
Time lost, money burned, opportunity missed.
Numbers when possible.
 
[THE LESSON — 2 to 3 lines]
What this pattern teaches.
Connect the specific story to the universal principle.
 
[CONCLUSION — punch line]
One strong closing statement.
The takeaway the reader carries with them.
\`\`\`
 
## Example Skeleton
 
\`\`\`
An IT project doesn't derail on its own.
It's poorly staffed.
Here's what happened.
 
A mid-market retailer, 200M revenue.
They needed to connect their ERP to a new PIM.
Timeline: 6 months. Budget: reasonable.
 
The consulting firm sent 3 juniors and a project manager
who had never touched a PIM integration.
Specs were vague. Nobody challenged them.
By month 4, the data model was wrong.
They started over from scratch on the mapping layer.
 
Result: 14 months instead of 6.
Budget doubled.
The internal team lost trust in the entire program.
 
The problem wasn't the technology.
It was putting profiles who couldn't challenge the specs
in charge of a project that required exactly that.
 
A motivated consultant delivers. A placed consultant executes.
\`\`\`
`,
 
  "library/templates/linkedin-post/myth-vs-reality.md": `---
name: myth-vs-reality
source: original
content-types: [linkedin-post]
best-for-pillars: [1, 2, 5]
best-for-angles: [contrarian, analytical]
date-created: 2026-02-24
---
 
# Myth vs. Reality Template
 
## When to Use
 
Use this template when a widely held belief in the market is actively causing harm. The myth/reality contrast creates immediate cognitive dissonance and positions the author as someone who sees what others miss. Strong for differentiation.
 
Best suited for:
- Debunking popular approaches (fake agile, replatforming as silver bullet)
- Correcting market misconceptions about architecture, AI, or tooling
- Challenging vendor narratives with field evidence
 
## Specs
 
- **Length**: 800-1,200 characters
- **Tone authority level**: High contrarian (confident challenger backed by evidence)
- **Visual rhythm**: Clear myth/reality separation, short blocks, punch line finish
- **Symbols allowed**: \`->\`, \`>\`, \`-\`
- **Emojis**: Zero
 
## Structure
 
\`\`\`
[HOOK — 3 lines, tension]
Line 1: <= 50 chars — The belief being challenged
Line 2: <= 50 chars — The twist or counter-signal
Line 3: <= 30 chars — Punch or pivot
 
[THE MYTH — 2 lines]
State what everyone believes.
Keep it recognizable -- the reader should nod along at first.
 
[THE REALITY — 3 to 5 lines]
Show what actually happens in the field.
Be specific: mechanisms, patterns, consequences.
Use concrete observations, not abstract claims.
 
[WHY IT'S DANGEROUS — 2 lines]
Explain the cost of believing the myth.
Connect it to a decision mistake.
 
[CONCLUSION — punch line]
One strong closing statement.
Reframe the entire topic in one sentence.
\`\`\`
 
## Example Skeleton
 
\`\`\`
A new PIM won't fix your data chaos.
Your process will.
Here's the difference.
 
The myth:
"If we implement a PIM, our product data problems disappear."
 
The reality:
A PIM is a container. If the data entering it is inconsistent,
the PIM just organizes the inconsistency faster.
Without governance -- who owns what, what workflow validates what --
you get the same mess with a nicer interface.
 
This is dangerous because companies spend 6-12 months
and 300K+ on a tool that mirrors their existing dysfunction.
 
The real fix isn't the tool. It's the governance around it.
\`\`\`
`,
